title: Stanford CS336 Language Modeling from Scratch | Spring 2026 | Lecture 12: Evaluation

segments.text:
So today, we're going to
talk about evaluation.

So far in this class
we've done everything we
need to train a language model.

We defined the architecture,
the optimizer, the main training
loop.

We showed how to make the
training fast with kernels,
parallelism.
And then we saw scaling laws.

We saw a little bit of how to
make inference fast as well.

So the only missing piece
now is talking about the data
that you train on.

So we're going to spend next
week talking about that.

And data is going to
shape the model behavior.

If you train on
code, then presumably

your model is going
to be good at code.

If you train only
on DNA sequences,
it probably can't speak English.

But before we even
get to data, we

need to talk about what
behavior we want from a model,

and that's going to be
the topic of evaluation.

So evaluation asks a
very simple question--

given the model that we've
trained, how good is it?
So what is good?

So evaluation might seem like
a fairly mechanical process.

You define some
prompts, you send

these prompts to your model,
you get back some responses,

and then you compute
the accuracy.

So why do we have a whole
lecture on evaluation?

But actually, evaluation
is a very deep topic,

and it's also very important
because evaluation is what
shapes the development of AI.

Evaluation sets North Stars
and model developers--

open, closed-- everyone
looks at evaluation
as a measure of progress.

So by thinking carefully
about your evaluation,

you implicitly shape
the development

of what your model is
going to be able to do.

So what's hard about
evaluation is the following.

So often, you start it with
an abstract construct of what
do I want my model to do?

Maybe I want my model to
be good at conversation
or good at reasoning.
OK.
So those are abstract concepts.

And then evaluation
is really the process

of turning that into a concrete
metric powered by, presumably,

some concrete prompts
or environments.

So this is going to
be the core challenge,

and we'll see many
examples of how

this process of abstract
construct, concrete metric
shows up.

So let's go back to a question
of what makes a model good.
Just kind of superficially.

Maybe a model is good if
it does well on benchmarks.

So there's this website,
Artificial Analysis,

which has become a bit of a
standard for thinking about

quote unquote,
"intelligence of models."

So if you go to
Artificial Analysis AI,

you can see a ranking of
different models from GPT-5.5,

all the way down-- well, there's
many more models after this
point.
And so this is a measure.

It includes a bunch of different
data sets, some of which
we'll talk about.

Or maybe a model is good if
it does well on benchmark

and is also cheap to run,
because cost should matter.

And this plot shows both
the intelligence index

from before, but
against how much money

it costs to run a particular
model based on inference cost.

And you can see, of course,
as you might expect,
that the two are correlated.

The more you pay to run, the
higher the intelligence index,
but it's not exactly aligned.

But maybe a model is good
if people just prefer it.

Later we'll also go a
little bit more into depth.

So this is the take that
Arena AI, formerly known
as Chatbot Arena, takes.

And you can form a
ranking based on models,

based on whether
people like it or not.

Or maybe a model is
good if people simply

choose to use it, and
therefore, pay for it.

So OpenRouter is
this service that

allows one to use
multiple different models
through a central endpoint.

And as a result,
they collect a lot

of statistics of what
models people are using.

And they publish
statistics, which

is really interesting
to look at,

the top bottles that are
used through OpenRouter.

So now, this is not
representative of all model

usage, but it gives you a sense
of what people on open router
are doing.

And so this is kind of
more of an economic lens.

I don't what good is, but
if people are paying for it,
it must be good.
OK.

So none of those are
necessarily the correct answer.

I don't know If there
is a correct answer,

but I'm just trying to get you
to think about different ways
of thinking about what good is.

So let's start now by looking at
different ways, concrete ways,
of evaluating language models.

And there's no better place
to start than going back

to the core idea of a
language model, which

is that it's a distribution P
of x over sequences of tokens.

And through that lens,
there's a very natural way

to evaluate a
distribution, which

is a perplexity, or
likelihood or log loss.

Those are all kind
of related concepts.

And it basically says, I
have a test data set d,

and I'm going to see how much
probability mass my language
model assigns to that d.

And I normalize it and
divide so that the numbers
are more interpretable.
But that's basically it.

How much mass does my
probability language model
assigned to a data set.
OK, so this is fairly natural.

In fact, when we
were doing training,

we minimized the perplexity
on the training set.

So the obvious thing to do
is measure the perplexity

on the test set, and this is
what people traditionally did

in language modeling research
for many years throughout
the 2010s.

If you pick up a
language modeling paper,
this is what they did.

And back then,
standard data sets

included the Penn
Treebank, WikiText-103.

There was also this One
Billion Word Benchmark,

which was taken from
machine translation sources.

And this is all in the
classic paradigm, which is
in-distribution of evaluation.

You train on some train
split of that data set,

and then you evaluate on some
test split of that data set.

And people actually
made a lot of progress,

which was just measured
in perplexity reduction.

So for example, there's
this famous paper

from 2016, which
showed that you could

apply CNNs and LSTMs to this
One Billion Word Benchmark

and get a huge
perplexity reduction.

And bear in mind,
around that time,

people were talking still
about n-gram models and maybe
hybrid models.

And this was the first
definitive result

that showed just pure neural
is clearly the way to go.

OK, so life was very
simple back then.
You train on the train split.

You test on a test
split for perplexity.

So then when GPT-2 came
around in 2019 from OpenAI,

they trained on
the data set called

WebText, which was 40
gigabytes of text websites
that were linked from Reddit.

And the way they evaluated
was actually different.

They evaluated zero-shot
on standard data sets

that everyone else
was evaluating on.

So this is out-of-distribution
evaluation.

So this is a table from their
paper, which show that as they
train different models.

So the biggest model is
1.5 billion parameters.

And they were able to show
quite a bit of improvement,

especially on the
small data sets.

So for example, PTB,
which is a tiny data set.

They were getting 35 perplexity
compared to the State
of The Art, which is 46.

Now, if you have enough
in-distribution data,

you don't quite do
as well as the SOTA.

But nonetheless, this
is kind of impressive,

given that this model was not
trained on One Billion Word's
data set at all.
I don't know.

Maybe there was some
overlap, because I

don't know if they did a
careful train test decontam.

And so it ushered
in a new paradigm

of thinking about language
models as not just

the in-distribution
setting, but how

we wanted to train
on a large data set

and evaluate on standard
benchmarks, which nowadays I

think has been very
common standard.

But back then, it was seen
as a bit of a novelty.

I will also argue, since we're
talking about perplexity, that
perplexity is you all need.

I wouldn't to take
this too seriously,

but I think it is
a mindset, I think,

that drives a lot of
language modeling research.

And the basic argument
goes like this.

So there's some true
distribution out there--
let's call it t--

and you're training
this model p.

So what is the best
perplexity you could obtain?

Well, the best
perplexity is the entropy

of that true
distribution, and that
is realized when p equals t.

So by minimizing
perplexity, you're basically
pushing down the perplexity.

And the only unique minimizer
is when you actually
get the true distribution.

And when you have the true
distribution, you're done.
You just model everything.

You can solve all
the problems, and you

can do things like
condition on the problem,
generate a solution.

You can condition on a
question, generate the answer.

So by pushing down
on perplexity,

we'll eventually reach
AGI, is the argument.

I mean, this might
sound, I don't know,
either a bit fantastical.

But in some sense,
this is really,

I think, a strong
driver of many people

who have been scaling and
scaling language models

at a time when, maybe, the
gains weren't as obvious.

Before GPT-3, I
think it wasn't clear

that language models were going
to have such a huge impact.

And it was really this belief
that, if you drove perplexity
down, good things will happen.

So of course, Perplexity
is maybe not all.

And one way to frame it is
that perplexity is maybe more
than you need.

So for example, if you have
a sentence like "Stanford was

founded in 1885,"
perplexity will say,

penalize the prediction
on all tokens.

So in particular, it'll say,
what is the probability of 1885,
given the rest?

And that token is
probably pretty useful.

It's basically a Q&A example,
phrased as sentence completion.

So it captures knowledge
about the world.

But other ones-- for example,
the first word of a sentence,
or even "founded"--

maybe not that
interesting, I think.
And perplexity doesn't care.

It's going to charge you bits
for every bit of a deviation
from the true distribution.

So there is a bit
of a solution here,

which is you can always measure
the conditional perplexity.

You can condition
on some prompt,

and then you can measure the
perplexity of the remaining
response.

So that allows you
to, in general,

focus on certain
tokens that you believe

to be relevant
and less on others
that are sort of incidental.
OK.

And also, it's
important to remember

that some benchmarks, even
though they are not explicitly

a perplexity
measure, are actually
just perplexity in disguise.

So, for example, if you
think about closed tasks.

There's this lambada paper
from, actually, a while ago,

2016, which is basically
fill in the blank.

And you have a context,
you have a sentence.

"Do you honestly think I will
want you to have a blank."

And the goal is to
figure out that word.

And even though this is
measured in terms of accuracy,

it is really a next-token
prediction problem.

And here, it's not
perplexity of all the words,
it's carefully chosen.

If you read this paper,
it's really carefully chosen

to be words, such
that you actually need

a bunch of long-contexts
to resolve.

So this is actually something
that the early GPT papers really

latched on to because they
were interested in long-context

modeling, thinking that
that was a key to unlocking
reasoning and all these things.

And really, by
focusing on positions

where, to resolve
that, it requires

long-distance
dependencies, is a way

to sharpen the perplexity
to some phenomenon
that you care about.

Here's another
popular classic data

set that you see in some
language modeling vowels.
HellaSwag.
So what is HellaSwag?

They went to various different
data sets and sources,

and came up with--
it's multiple choice,

but it's really
sentence completion.

And at some level,
it's perplexity.
So here's a sentence.

"A woman is outside
with a bucket and a dog.
The dog is running around.
She blank."

And then you're trying
to fill in which
sentence completes it the best.

So it's not exactly perplexity,
it's multiple choice.

But in some ways,
it's perplexity.
So one word of warning.

If you ever decide that
you're sold on perplexity

as, really, the
measure and you're

going to launch a leaderboard
and have people compete

against perplexity,
just a word of caution

is that, what is a perplexity
leaderboard look like?

So people are going to submit
a language model to you,

and you have to
compute a logprob.

You send them the test data, and
they give you back a logprob.

Well, you look at
that and say, hmm,

that might be a little
bit fishy because you

need to trust that the
probabilities they hand back

to you are valid and
that they sum to 1.

Otherwise, I could
just have implement LM,

and it always returns 1
or, I guess, logprob 0.

And I would get very
good perplexity,

but clearly, that's not
a valid distribution.

And so with this
contract it's not

really easy to check
that someone is actually

internally having
a log probability,
so there's some trust.

Maybe you have to look
at the code or something.

For downstream tasks, this
is much more straightforward

because you can
have a black box LM.

I give you a prompt,
you give me a response,

and I can just use that response
and calculate some accuracy.

So perplexity is
fundamentally different

because it deals
with distributions,

and you have to
be a bit careful.

And not to mention,
there's lots of problems
with autoregressive models.

But if you have VAEs or there's
some models where you can't even

compute the distribution--
you can compute only a bound--

then you have to really actually
trust the math as well that this
is actually a valid bound.
OK.

So The summary of perplexity
is that it is actually

still used quite heavily in
language model development.
It has some nice properties.

We saw that it's generally
used for scaling laws

because it's also
smoothly varying

with scale, which is important
for getting scaling laws.

But, at the end of the day,
especially if you're not

a believer, then you need some
benchmarks to really capture

real world situations,
to convince people

that your language
model is good.

For the believers, if I show you
a remarkably slow perplexity,
they'll be convinced.

OK, any questions
about perplexity?
OK.

Hopefully, everyone's
a believer now.

OK, so let's talk
about exam benchmarks.

So exams, as all of
your students know,

is a way that we
use to test humans.

And so you can adopt
the same mentality
to test language models.

The nice thing about
exams is that you

have a very careful
control over the subject,
over the difficulty.

You can design them to have
unambiguous correct answers,
which makes them easy to grade.

So there's a lot of nice
properties about exams.

And so, for this reason, a
lot of LM benchmarking culture

was based on just
this idea of exams.

So obviously, there's
been a lot of benchmarks,

but I'll just highlight
some of the ones

that I think have
been influential

in the development
of language models.

So this one is MMLU, or Massive
Multitask Language Understanding
from Hendrycks et al, 2020.

And at that time,
it wasn't clear

that language models were
really like general purpose task
solvers.

This was around the time

Many people, I think, still
thought of language models
as, well, language models.
You generate fluent English.

But they had this

let's just try to be

We're going to go

we're going to have

And despite the name MMLU,

Understanding, it's

knowledge and reasoning

And the way they evaluated

So they essentially said,
I have formed this prompt.

The following are questions

Some in-context examples of

But at that time

pretty radical that

this fairly complicated question

expect a language

model to actually do
something reasonable.

And sure enough, at the outset,
the models were not great,
I would say.
